stem: A 31-year-old woman attends a Central Coast LHD GP practice with 8 months of persistent worry about her finances, her partner's health, her children's safety at school, and her own performance at work. She reports muscle tension, sleep-onset insomnia, restlessness and irritability most days. She occasionally feels her heart racing during the day when worry intensifies but has never had a discrete episode lasting under 10 minutes with peak fear, dyspnoea and depersonalisation. She avoids no specific situations and has no intrusive obsessions. She has had two normal thyroid function tests in the past 6 months.

data_table.Examination: Anxious-appearing; resting HR 88, BP 122/78; otherwise unremarkable.
data_table.Investigations: TSH 1.8 mU/L (RR 0.4-4.0); B12 350 pmol/L; ECG sinus rhythm.

lead_in: Which is the single most likely diagnosis?

options.text: Generalised anxiety disorder (GAD)
options.rationale: DSM-5 generalised anxiety disorder requires excessive worry on more days than not for at least 6 months across multiple domains, difficulty controlling worry, and at least three of six symptoms (restlessness, fatigue, concentration difficulty, irritability, muscle tension, sleep disturbance). This vignette meets duration, multi-domain worry, and symptom criteria. The absence of discrete peak-fear episodes (panic), specific avoidance (phobia or social anxiety), and intrusive obsessions excludes the alternatives.

options.text: Panic disorder
options.rationale: Panic disorder requires recurrent unexpected discrete panic attacks (peak within minutes) with anticipatory worry; this patient has no discrete attacks. Would be wrong (Source: DSM-5-TR).

options.text: Social anxiety disorder
options.rationale: Social anxiety requires marked fear of social or performance situations with avoidance; this patient avoids no specific situations. Would be wrong (Source: DSM-5-TR).

options.text: Obsessive-compulsive disorder
options.rationale: OCD requires intrusive ego-dystonic obsessions and compulsions; none described. Worry in GAD is recognisable as 'mine' and tied to plausible content. Would be wrong (Source: DSM-5-TR).

options.text: Hyperthyroidism
options.rationale: Normal TSH on two recent occasions excludes hyperthyroidism. Would be wrong (Source: eTG Endocrinology).

explanation.summary: GAD is characterised by excessive multi-domain worry on more days than not for at least 6 months with somatic anxiety symptoms; it lacks the discrete autonomic-peak attacks of panic, the situational specificity of phobia, and the intrusive ego-dystonic content of OCD. First-line management combines psychological therapy (CBT) with an SSRI or SNRI; benzodiazepines are not first-line maintenance therapy.
key_points: GAD = >=6 months excessive multi-domain worry + >=3 of 6 somatic symptoms
Panic disorder = discrete unexpected attacks + anticipatory worry
Social anxiety = situational fear with avoidance
OCD = ego-dystonic intrusive obsessions + compulsions
Exclude hyperthyroidism and substance use before diagnosis
explanation.pearls: Worry content in GAD is recognised as one's own and is plausible (finances, kids, work); intrusive obsessions in OCD are ego-dystonic, unwanted and often bizarre.

subtopic_detail: GAD diagnostic discrimination from panic, social anxiety, OCD

stem: A 34-year-old man with newly diagnosed generalised anxiety disorder presents to his GP at a Hunter New England LHD community practice. Symptoms have persisted despite 6 months of attempted self-help and a course of CBT through MindSpot, with PHQ-9 7 and GAD-7 16. He has no past psychiatric admission, no substance use, no QT-prolonging drugs, and no contraindications to standard antidepressants. He works as a construction supervisor and asks for an effective but non-sedating option.

data_table.Examination: Anxious, HR 92, BP 128/80, otherwise unremarkable.
data_table.Investigations: TFT, U&E, FBC normal; ECG sinus rhythm with QTc 410 ms.

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate initial pharmacological choice?

options.text: Escitalopram 10 mg daily, titrating after 2 weeks if tolerated
options.rationale: Australian (eTG, RANZCP) and international guidance position SSRIs as first-line pharmacotherapy for GAD; escitalopram has Level I evidence, a clean drug-interaction profile, low sedation burden suitable for a safety-critical occupation, and titratable dosing. SNRIs (venlafaxine, duloxetine) are equally effective alternatives.

options.text: Diazepam 5 mg twice daily long-term
options.rationale: Benzodiazepines are not first-line maintenance therapy for GAD due to tolerance, dependence, sedation impairing work, and accident risk in safety-critical roles. Would be wrong long-term (Source: eTG).

options.text: Quetiapine 50 mg nocte
options.rationale: Low-dose quetiapine has limited evidence, off-label status, sedation, metabolic risk and is not first-line. Would be wrong (Source: RANZCP).

options.text: Propranolol 40 mg twice daily
options.rationale: Propranolol addresses peripheral autonomic symptoms (tremor, tachycardia) in performance anxiety, not the cognitive worry of GAD. Would be wrong as monotherapy for GAD (Source: AMH).

options.text: Pregabalin 75 mg twice daily
options.rationale: Pregabalin has evidence in GAD and is used overseas but in Australia is positioned as second-line due to abuse potential, sedation, weight gain and PBS restrictions. Would be wrong as initial choice (Source: eTG).

explanation.summary: First-line pharmacotherapy for GAD in Australia is an SSRI or SNRI, alongside or after psychological therapy (CBT). Escitalopram, sertraline, venlafaxine and duloxetine all have evidence. Benzodiazepines are reserved for short-term symptom relief during titration of the antidepressant or for acute crises; long-term use is avoided. Pregabalin and quetiapine are second-line. Propranolol has a niche role for autonomic peripheral symptoms (performance-anxiety subtype), not cognitive worry.
key_points: First-line: SSRI or SNRI
CBT continues alongside pharmacotherapy
Avoid long-term benzodiazepine maintenance
Pregabalin and quetiapine are second-line
Beta-blockers are not GAD-specific
explanation.pearls: SSRIs in anxiety often start at HALF the depression starting dose because of an initial anxiety paradox in week 1; warn the patient to expect transient worsening.

subtopic_detail: First-line pharmacotherapy choice for GAD

stem: A 26-year-old woman presents to a Central Coast LHD ED with a 25-minute episode of acute chest tightness, palpitations, dyspnoea, perioral paraesthesia, depersonalisation and a fear of dying that began suddenly while at her desk at work. She has had three similar episodes in the last 2 months and now avoids meetings. ECG is normal, troponin negative, D-dimer negative. She is calm by the time she is reviewed.

data_table.Examination: Now settled, HR 76, BP 118/72, no chest wall tenderness.
data_table.Investigations: ECG sinus rhythm, troponin negative, D-dimer negative, TSH 2.1 mU/L.

lead_in: Which intervention has the strongest evidence base for long-term reduction in panic disorder symptoms?

options.text: Cognitive behavioural therapy with interoceptive exposure
options.rationale: CBT with interoceptive exposure (deliberately provoking feared bodily sensations to learn they are not dangerous) has the strongest long-term evidence in panic disorder, with effect sizes equal to or greater than pharmacotherapy and lower relapse rates after cessation. RANZCP and NICE position CBT as first-line; SSRIs are equally appropriate where therapy is unavailable or declined.
source_refs: NICE Generalised Anxiety and Panic CG113
Pompoli A et al. Cochrane review CBT for panic 2018

options.text: Long-term diazepam 5 mg twice daily
options.rationale: Long-term benzodiazepine carries tolerance, dependence and rebound, and is not the long-term strategy. Would be wrong (Source: eTG).

options.text: Beta-blocker as monotherapy
options.rationale: Beta-blockers address tachycardia and tremor but do not treat the cognitive component or prevent panic; not first-line. Would be wrong (Source: NICE).

options.text: Avoidance of all situations that have triggered attacks
options.rationale: Avoidance reinforces panic disorder through negative reinforcement; CBT actively counters avoidance. Would be wrong (Source: NICE).

options.text: Reassurance alone after normal cardiac workup
options.rationale: Reassurance after cardiac workup is necessary but insufficient; without active intervention panic disorder typically persists. Would be wrong (Source: RANZCP).

explanation.summary: Panic disorder responds robustly to CBT incorporating cognitive restructuring, interoceptive exposure (provoking feared sensations to extinguish the threat appraisal) and behavioural exposure to avoided situations. SSRIs are equally first-line pharmacologically. Combined CBT plus SSRI is not consistently superior to either alone and has more side effects. Benzodiazepines may be used briefly during SSRI titration but are not the long-term plan.
key_points: CBT with interoceptive exposure is first-line psychological therapy
SSRI or SNRI first-line pharmacotherapy
Avoidance is the maintaining factor
Brief benzodiazepine bridge only during SSRI initiation
Reassurance after cardiac workup is necessary but insufficient
explanation.pearls: Interoceptive exposure exercises include hyperventilation (paraesthesia + dizziness), spinning in a chair (depersonalisation), and breathing through a straw (dyspnoea); the goal is learning that sensation does not equal danger.

sources.label: Pompoli A et al. Cochrane Database 2018

subtopic_detail: Long-term first-line management of panic disorder

stem: A 24-year-old violinist preparing for a major audition presents to her GP at a Central Coast LHD primary care clinic. She becomes severely tremulous and tachycardic during performances, which interferes with her bowing. She does not have generalised social anxiety, does not avoid social situations outside of performance, and is otherwise well. She asks for a non-sedating option that she can take an hour before the audition.

data_table.Examination: Anxious-appearing about audition; otherwise unremarkable. Resting HR 76, BP 118/72.
data_table.Investigations: ECG sinus rhythm, no QT prolongation; no asthma history.

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate medication?

options.text: Propranolol 20-40 mg orally one hour before the audition
options.rationale: Performance-only social anxiety responds well to a one-off dose of a beta-blocker (commonly propranolol 20-40 mg) taken 30-60 minutes prior. It blocks peripheral adrenergic symptoms (tremor, tachycardia, sweating) that directly interfere with motor performance, without sedation. Generalised social anxiety disorder is treated differently (SSRI plus CBT).
source_refs: Stein DJ & Stein MB. Social anxiety disorder. Lancet 2008

options.text: Sertraline 50 mg daily ongoing
options.rationale: SSRIs treat generalised social anxiety disorder, not isolated performance anxiety; daily ongoing dosing is not indicated for this presentation. Would be wrong (Source: eTG).

options.text: Diazepam 5 mg orally one hour before the audition
options.rationale: Benzodiazepines blunt fine motor control and cognition, which would impair the audition; sedation also unwanted. Would be wrong (Source: AMH).

options.text: Quetiapine 25 mg one hour before
options.rationale: Sedating, no evidence for performance anxiety, would impair motor performance. Would be wrong (Source: AMH).

options.text: Mirtazapine 15 mg the night before
options.rationale: Sedating antidepressant with no evidence for performance anxiety; not indicated. Would be wrong (Source: AMH).

explanation.summary: Performance-only social anxiety (a specifier within social anxiety disorder) responds well to a single pre-event dose of a beta-blocker (propranolol 20-40 mg orally 30-60 min prior) which blocks peripheral autonomic symptoms without sedation. Generalised social anxiety disorder is a different entity, managed with SSRI plus CBT. Beta-blockers do not help cognitive components and are not generalised-SAD treatment.
key_points: Performance-only SAD: PRN propranolol 20-40 mg
Generalised SAD: SSRI + CBT
Beta-blocker addresses peripheral symptoms not cognitive worry
Avoid benzodiazepines (sedation, motor blunting)
Screen for asthma, bradyarrhythmia before propranolol
explanation.pearls: A test dose at a low-stakes occasion (rehearsal) is wise to identify tolerability before the high-stakes performance.

subtopic_detail: Propranolol for performance-only social anxiety

stem: A 19-year-old university student presents to her GP at a Hunter New England LHD student health clinic. She describes 2 hours per day of intrusive thoughts that she might have left the stove on, the door unlocked, or contaminated her hands. She returns home repeatedly to check the stove (typically 3-4 times before being able to leave) and washes her hands until they are raw. She finds the thoughts distressing, recognises them as her own and excessive, and tries to resist them without success.

data_table.Examination: Hands erythematous from over-washing; otherwise unremarkable.
data_table.Investigations: Nil acute required.

lead_in: Which feature most strongly supports a diagnosis of obsessive-compulsive disorder over an anxious worry pattern?

options.text: The thoughts are ego-dystonic, recognised as excessive, and accompanied by compulsive rituals performed to neutralise them
options.rationale: Ego-dystonic intrusions (recognised as unwanted, excessive, and inconsistent with the person's values) accompanied by ritualised neutralising behaviours are the diagnostic core of OCD per DSM-5. GAD worries are ego-syntonic, recognisable as 'mine', plausible in content, and not neutralised by ritual. Insight in OCD distinguishes it from psychosis.
source_refs: Stein DJ et al. OCD review Lancet 2019

options.text: Hand erythema from over-washing
options.rationale: Physical sequelae of compulsion support the diagnosis indirectly but are not diagnostic core; many conditions cause hand erythema. Would be less specific (Source: DSM-5-TR).

options.text: The duration of symptoms (less than 6 months)
options.rationale: OCD has no duration threshold like GAD; the disorder is defined by symptom characteristics not chronicity. Would be wrong (Source: DSM-5-TR).

options.text: The young age at presentation
options.rationale: OCD onset is bimodal (childhood and early adulthood) but age does not define the diagnosis. Would be wrong (Source: DSM-5-TR).

options.text: The presence of intrusive worries about realistic risks (stove, door)
options.rationale: Worry about realistic risks is non-specific and present in GAD too; what distinguishes OCD is the ego-dystonic intrusion + compulsion. Would be wrong (Source: DSM-5-TR).

explanation.summary: OCD is defined by obsessions (recurrent intrusive ego-dystonic thoughts, urges or images) and compulsions (repetitive behaviours or mental acts performed to neutralise the obsession), causing distress or functional impairment (usually >1 hour/day). Insight is generally preserved (distinguishing from psychosis). GAD worries are ego-syntonic and not ritualised; PTSD intrusions are tied to a specific trauma.
key_points: OCD = ego-dystonic obsessions + ritualised compulsions
GAD worry is ego-syntonic, plausible, not ritualised
Insight is typically preserved (distinguishes from psychosis)
Common themes: contamination, harm, symmetry, taboo
>=1 hour/day or functional impairment threshold
explanation.pearls: Pure-O OCD (intrusive thoughts without overt compulsion) involves mental rituals; the absence of visible compulsion does not exclude OCD.

sources.label: Stein DJ et al. OCD. Lancet 2019

subtopic_detail: Ego-dystonic intrusions + ritual = OCD

stem: A 28-year-old man with OCD (contamination subtype with 4-hour daily washing) attends a Hunter New England LHD outpatient psychology service. His Yale-Brown Obsessive Compulsive Scale (Y-BOCS) score is 24 (severe). He asks what the most effective psychological treatment will involve.

data_table.Examination: Erythematous excoriated hands.
data_table.Investigations: Nil acute.

lead_in: Which description most accurately describes the first-line psychological intervention?

options.text: Exposure and response prevention: graded exposure to feared stimuli while refraining from compulsive rituals, allowing habituation and disconfirmation of feared outcomes
options.rationale: Exposure and Response Prevention (ERP) is the highest-evidence first-line psychological intervention for OCD. It combines graded in-vivo exposure to feared stimuli (e.g. touching a doorknob) with deliberate prevention of the compulsive ritual (e.g. not washing afterwards). The mechanism is habituation and inhibitory learning - new safety learning competes with the threat appraisal. Effect sizes typically exceed pharmacotherapy alone for OCD.
source_refs: Abramowitz JS. ERP for OCD review

options.text: Supportive psychotherapy with focus on insight into childhood origins
options.rationale: Insight-oriented therapy alone has limited evidence in OCD; ERP outperforms. Would be wrong as first-line (Source: NICE).

options.text: Generic CBT focused on thought monitoring without behavioural exposure
options.rationale: Without behavioural exposure component, cognitive-only intervention is less effective; ERP is the specific protocol. Would be wrong (Source: NICE).

options.text: Acceptance and commitment therapy as monotherapy
options.rationale: ACT may have a role as an alternative or adjunct but ERP remains first-line evidence. Would be wrong as the specific first-line intervention (Source: RANZCP).

options.text: EMDR targeting initial onset of compulsions
options.rationale: EMDR is a PTSD-targeted therapy without OCD evidence base. Would be wrong (Source: RANZCP).

explanation.summary: Exposure and Response Prevention (ERP) is the highest-evidence psychological treatment for OCD. Graded exposure to feared stimuli + deliberate prevention of compulsions builds inhibitory learning that competes with the threat appraisal. Pharmacotherapy first-line is SSRI (sertraline, fluoxetine, fluvoxamine or paroxetine) at often higher doses than for depression. Combined ERP + SSRI is recommended for moderate-severe OCD.
key_points: ERP = first-line psychological therapy for OCD
Graded exposure + ritual prevention
Mechanism: inhibitory learning / habituation
SSRI pharmacotherapy often at higher doses than depression
Combined ERP + SSRI for moderate-severe (Y-BOCS >=16)
explanation.pearls: ERP is hard work; about 25% of patients decline or drop out; engagement and rapport building before formal exposure is critical.

subtopic_detail: ERP as first-line psychological therapy for OCD

stem: A 35-year-old woman with OCD (Y-BOCS 26) has been on sertraline 100 mg daily for 10 weeks with minimal response. She is engaged with ERP weekly with a clinical psychologist. The treating psychiatrist is considering dose adjustment.

data_table.Examination: Persistent contamination obsessions with washing rituals; minimal change from baseline.
data_table.Investigations: ECG sinus rhythm with QTc 420 ms; no medical contraindications.

lead_in: Which adjustment most aligns with evidence-based OCD pharmacotherapy?

options.text: Increase sertraline progressively toward 200 mg daily, allowing 10-12 weeks at the higher dose before judging response
options.rationale: OCD typically requires higher SSRI doses than depression (sertraline up to 200 mg daily, fluoxetine up to 80 mg, fluvoxamine up to 300 mg) and a longer 10-12 week trial at maximum tolerated dose before judging response. RANZCP and NICE guidance support this. Switching to another SSRI or adding atypical antipsychotic augmentation are next steps after an adequate trial.

options.text: Switch to amitriptyline 75 mg nocte
options.rationale: Amitriptyline (tricyclic) is not first-line for OCD; clomipramine has OCD evidence but amitriptyline does not. Would be wrong (Source: eTG).

options.text: Add diazepam 5 mg twice daily
options.rationale: Benzodiazepines are not effective for OCD core symptoms; they treat associated anxiety only and may impair ERP learning. Would be wrong (Source: NICE).

options.text: Switch to a benzodiazepine and reassess in 2 weeks
options.rationale: Same issue; not OCD-treatment. Would be wrong (Source: NICE).

options.text: Discontinue sertraline and rely on ERP alone
options.rationale: In moderate-severe OCD (Y-BOCS 26), combined treatment is recommended; abandoning pharmacotherapy reduces overall benefit. Would be wrong (Source: RANZCP).

explanation.summary: OCD pharmacotherapy differs from depression: higher SSRI doses, longer adequate trial (10-12 weeks at maximum tolerated dose). After an adequate trial without response, options include switching to another SSRI, switching to clomipramine, or augmenting with a low-dose atypical antipsychotic (risperidone, aripiprazole). Combined SSRI + ERP outperforms either alone for moderate-severe OCD.
key_points: OCD requires higher SSRI doses than depression
Adequate trial = 10-12 weeks at max tolerated dose
Sertraline up to 200 mg, fluoxetine up to 80 mg, fluvoxamine up to 300 mg
Clomipramine is the tricyclic with OCD evidence
Combined SSRI + ERP preferred for moderate-severe
explanation.pearls: Clomipramine has the largest effect size in OCD meta-analyses but is positioned second-line due to anticholinergic, cardiac and seizure burden; SSRIs are preferred first-line for tolerability.

subtopic_detail: OCD SSRI dose optimisation and trial duration

stem: A 32-year-old veteran presents to a Hunter New England LHD GP 8 months after returning from deployment. He reports intrusive memories of an IED incident triggered by sudden loud noises, vivid nightmares occurring 3-4 nights a week, avoidance of car parks (where the incident occurred), persistent negative beliefs that 'I should have died with them', hypervigilance, exaggerated startle, and sleep disturbance. Symptoms have been present since deployment and have not improved.

data_table.Examination: Mood low, hyperalert, startles at door slam, no perceptual abnormality.
data_table.Investigations: TFT, FBC normal; urine drug screen negative.

lead_in: Which DSM-5 criterion cluster set is most clearly met in this presentation?

options.text: Intrusion + avoidance + negative cognitions and mood + altered arousal and reactivity, persisting more than 1 month after exposure
options.rationale: DSM-5 PTSD requires exposure to a Criterion A traumatic event followed by at least one intrusion symptom (memories, nightmares, flashbacks), at least one persistent avoidance, at least two negative alterations in cognition and mood, at least two alterations in arousal and reactivity, duration >1 month and functional impairment. This vignette describes symptoms in all four clusters present for 8 months.

options.text: Persistent hallucinations without insight
options.rationale: PTSD intrusions are ego-syntonic re-experiencing with preserved insight, not hallucinations; this describes a different presentation. Would be wrong (Source: DSM-5-TR).

options.text: Dissociative identity states without trauma exposure
options.rationale: PTSD criterion A requires trauma exposure; this option omits trauma. Would be wrong (Source: DSM-5-TR).

options.text: Discrete panic attacks with anticipatory worry only
options.rationale: This describes panic disorder, not PTSD; absent are the intrusion, avoidance, negative cognitions and arousal clusters tied to trauma. Would be wrong (Source: DSM-5-TR).

options.text: Compulsive checking rituals without obsessions
options.rationale: This describes OCD-spectrum, not PTSD. Would be wrong (Source: DSM-5-TR).

explanation.summary: DSM-5 PTSD requires Criterion A trauma exposure + symptoms across four clusters: (B) intrusion - memories, nightmares, flashbacks; (C) avoidance - internal or external reminders; (D) negative alterations in cognition and mood - negative beliefs, distorted blame, persistent negative emotion, anhedonia, detachment; (E) alterations in arousal and reactivity - irritability, recklessness, hypervigilance, startle, sleep disturbance. Duration >1 month, functional impairment, not attributable to substance or medical condition.
key_points: Four symptom clusters: intrusion / avoidance / negative cognition+mood / arousal
Duration >1 month (acute stress disorder if <1 month)
Functional impairment required
Criterion A trauma exposure required
Dissociative subtype if depersonalisation/derealisation prominent
explanation.pearls: Complex PTSD (ICD-11) adds disturbances in self-organisation (affect dysregulation, negative self-concept, interpersonal difficulties) on top of the core PTSD symptoms, typically after prolonged or repeated trauma.

subtopic_detail: DSM-5 PTSD four-cluster diagnostic structure

stem: A 28-year-old woman with PTSD following sexual assault 18 months ago is referred to a Central Coast LHD psychology service. She is engaged, not currently using alcohol or other substances, and is asking for the most effective psychological treatment.

data_table.Examination: PCL-5 score 52; no current self-harm; no active substance use.
data_table.Investigations: Nil.

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate first-line psychological treatment per Australian and international guidelines?

options.text: Trauma-focused cognitive behavioural therapy (TF-CBT), such as Cognitive Processing Therapy or Prolonged Exposure
options.rationale: Phoenix Australia Guidelines 2020 (the national PTSD guideline) and NICE NG116 position trauma-focused psychological therapies as first-line: TF-CBT (including Cognitive Processing Therapy and Prolonged Exposure) and EMDR. These have the strongest evidence base. Non-trauma-focused supportive therapy is not equivalent. SSRIs are equally first-line where psychological therapy is unavailable or declined.
source_refs: Forbes D et al. Aust N Z J Psychiatry 2020

options.text: Supportive counselling without trauma processing, with weekly sessions for 12 weeks via a registered psychologist under Better Access
options.rationale: Non-trauma-focused supportive counselling has weaker evidence and is not first-line for chronic PTSD; trauma-focused therapy is preferred (Source: Phoenix Australia).

options.text: Critical incident stress debriefing in a single session targeting the index event, delivered within 6 weeks of the original assault
options.rationale: Single-session debriefing in the immediate post-event period has not been shown to prevent and may worsen PTSD; not indicated 18 months after the event (Source: Phoenix Australia).

options.text: Long-term psychodynamic psychotherapy as monotherapy with fortnightly sessions for 12 months without structured trauma-focused techniques
options.rationale: Psychodynamic therapy has limited evidence for PTSD compared with trauma-focused CBT and EMDR; not first-line for chronic PTSD (Source: NICE NG116).

options.text: Clinical hypnotherapy as monotherapy with eight weekly sessions targeting traumatic memory recall and relaxation responses
options.rationale: Hypnotherapy is not a recommended first-line PTSD treatment and is not supported by Australian or NICE guidance (Source: Phoenix Australia; NICE NG116).

explanation.summary: First-line treatments for PTSD per Phoenix Australia 2020 are trauma-focused psychological therapies (TF-CBT including Cognitive Processing Therapy and Prolonged Exposure, and EMDR) and SSRIs (sertraline, paroxetine). Single-session critical incident debriefing in the immediate post-event period is contraindicated. Non-trauma-focused therapies have weaker evidence and are not first-line.
key_points: First-line psychological: TF-CBT (CPT, PE) or EMDR
First-line pharmacological: sertraline or paroxetine
CISD not recommended and may harm
Non-trauma-focused therapies weaker
Combined approach for moderate-severe presentations
explanation.pearls: Cognitive Processing Therapy emphasises challenging trauma-related cognitions (stuck points); Prolonged Exposure emphasises in vivo + imaginal exposure; EMDR uses bilateral stimulation alongside trauma recall - all three trauma-focused approaches have comparable effect sizes.

subtopic_detail: First-line trauma-focused psychological therapy for PTSD

stem: A 47-year-old man with PTSD following a motor vehicle collision attends a Hunter New England LHD veterans' mental health clinic. He is on sertraline 100 mg daily and engaged with TF-CBT but reports persistent severe nightmares 4-5 nights per week, often with awakening drenched in sweat. His sleep is unrefreshing and his daytime function is impaired. His BP is 128/76 supine and 116/72 standing, and his ECG is unremarkable.

data_table.Examination: Mood low-stable, no current self-harm.
data_table.Investigations: ECG sinus rhythm; BP 128/76 supine, 116/72 standing.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate adjunctive pharmacotherapy for his nightmares?

options.text: Prazosin 1 mg at bedtime, titrating cautiously by clinical response and orthostatic tolerance
options.rationale: Prazosin (alpha-1 adrenergic antagonist) reduces PTSD-related nightmares; early studies (Raskind et al) showed strong effect, with later trials more mixed. Phoenix Australia and VA/DoD guidelines retain prazosin as an option for PTSD nightmares. Australian practice starts at 1 mg nocte (first-dose hypotension warning), titrating to 5-10 mg by clinical response, with monitoring of orthostatic BP. The mechanism is thought to involve reduced central noradrenergic activity during REM.
source_refs: Raskind MA et al. NEJM 2018 PACT trial

options.text: Add zolpidem 10 mg nocte long-term
options.rationale: Z-drugs do not treat PTSD nightmares and carry dependence and complex-sleep-behaviour risks. Would be wrong (Source: AMH).

options.text: Add quetiapine 100 mg nocte
options.rationale: Quetiapine for sleep has metabolic and sedation risks without strong PTSD-nightmare-specific evidence. Would be wrong as targeted nightmare therapy (Source: AMH).

options.text: Add diazepam 5 mg nocte
options.rationale: Benzodiazepines worsen PTSD long-term, may impair trauma extinction learning, and are not recommended. Would be wrong (Source: Phoenix Australia).

options.text: Add a beta-blocker (propranolol 40 mg nocte)
options.rationale: Propranolol has been investigated for PTSD but lacks specific evidence for nightmares and is not the recommended agent. Would be wrong (Source: Phoenix Australia).

explanation.summary: Prazosin (alpha-1 antagonist) is the most-studied targeted pharmacotherapy for PTSD nightmares, with positive effect in earlier trials (Raskind 2003, 2013) and more mixed results in the larger PACT trial (Raskind NEJM 2018), but retained as an option in Phoenix Australia and VA/DoD guidelines. Start 1 mg nocte (first-dose hypotension), titrate cautiously to clinical effect (typically 5-10 mg), monitoring orthostatic BP. Mechanism: central noradrenergic blockade during REM.
key_points: Prazosin first-line targeted for PTSD nightmares
Start 1 mg nocte; first-dose hypotension warning
Titrate to clinical effect; usually 5-10 mg
Monitor orthostatic BP
Avoid benzodiazepines and Z-drugs long-term in PTSD
explanation.pearls: Image rehearsal therapy (a psychological intervention rewriting nightmare content) is the corresponding non-drug intervention with positive evidence.

sources.label: Raskind MA et al. PACT trial NEJM 2018

subtopic_detail: Prazosin titration for PTSD nightmares

stem: A 24-year-old man presents to his GP at a Central Coast LHD primary care clinic 10 days after surviving a serious motor vehicle collision in which a passenger died. He reports intrusive memories, nightmares, hypervigilance, avoidance of driving and impaired sleep, and feels emotionally numb. Symptoms have been continuous since the event.

data_table.Examination: Mood low-anxious, no perceptual abnormality, no current intent.
data_table.Investigations: Nil acute.

lead_in: Which is the most accurate diagnostic label and the recommended initial intervention?

options.text: Acute stress disorder; offer trauma-focused CBT and assess at 4 weeks for transition to PTSD
options.rationale: Symptoms within the first 3 days to 1 month after trauma exposure are classified as acute stress disorder under DSM-5; persistence beyond 1 month transitions to PTSD. Phoenix Australia Guidelines support trauma-focused CBT in this acute window for patients with significant symptoms (not single-session debriefing). Watchful waiting alone is appropriate for milder presentations.

options.text: PTSD; offer immediate trauma-focused CBT
options.rationale: Duration of symptoms is less than 1 month, so the DSM-5 label is acute stress disorder, not PTSD. Would be wrong on diagnostic label (Source: DSM-5-TR).

options.text: Adjustment disorder; provide supportive counselling
options.rationale: Adjustment disorder applies to less severe identifiable stressor responses without the intrusion/arousal/avoidance cluster pattern; this presentation fits acute stress disorder. Would be wrong (Source: DSM-5-TR).

options.text: Normal grief; reassure and review in 4 weeks
options.rationale: Functional impairment plus full symptom cluster exceeds normal grief; intervention is indicated. Would be wrong (Source: DSM-5-TR).

options.text: Critical incident stress debriefing in a single session this week
options.rationale: CISD is not recommended and may worsen outcomes; targeted trauma-focused CBT is the evidence-based option. Would be wrong (Source: Phoenix Australia).

explanation.summary: Acute stress disorder (DSM-5) describes the trauma-response cluster appearing 3 days to 1 month after exposure; PTSD applies when symptoms persist beyond 1 month. Trauma-focused CBT delivered in the acute window is recommended for clinically significant symptoms; single-session debriefing is not. Watchful waiting is appropriate for milder presentations. SSRIs are not first-line in the acute window.
key_points: Acute stress disorder: 3 days to 1 month post-trauma
PTSD: >1 month
TF-CBT recommended in acute window for clinically significant symptoms
Watchful waiting for milder presentations
CISD contraindicated
explanation.pearls: Approximately 50% of ASD progresses to PTSD if untreated; early TF-CBT reduces this progression.

subtopic_detail: Acute stress disorder vs PTSD diagnostic threshold and intervention

stem: A 38-year-old woman with severe needle phobia is referred to a Hunter New England LHD psychology service. She has avoided dental treatment for 8 years, declined vaccinations, and her phobia is now interfering with her need for blood tests for a chronic medical condition. She experiences pre-syncopal symptoms with vasovagal syncope on previous attempts at venepuncture.

data_table.Examination: Anxious about appointment topic; otherwise unremarkable.
data_table.Investigations: Nil.

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate first-line treatment, and which modification addresses her specific subtype?

options.text: Graded in-vivo exposure with applied tension technique to prevent vasovagal syncope
options.rationale: Specific phobia (blood-injection-injury subtype) is uniquely characterised by a vasovagal response (initial sympathetic spike then parasympathetic-mediated drop in BP and HR leading to syncope). The targeted treatment is graded in-vivo exposure plus applied tension (Ost's protocol: repeatedly tensing large muscle groups to elevate BP and prevent syncope during exposure). This has the strongest evidence for this subtype.

options.text: Diazepam 10 mg before the procedure
options.rationale: Benzodiazepine may reduce sympathetic component but does not prevent the parasympathetic vasovagal drop and does not extinguish the phobia. Would be wrong as the targeted treatment (Source: RANZCP).

options.text: SSRI long-term as monotherapy
options.rationale: SSRIs are not first-line for specific phobia; exposure-based therapy is. Would be wrong (Source: NICE).

options.text: Beta-blocker before the procedure
options.rationale: Beta-blocker blunts the initial sympathetic spike but may worsen the subsequent vasovagal hypotension and does not extinguish the phobia. Would be wrong (Source: AMH).

options.text: EMDR as monotherapy
options.rationale: EMDR is not first-line for specific phobia; exposure-based therapy is. Would be wrong (Source: RANZCP).

explanation.summary: Specific phobia is treated first-line with graded in-vivo exposure; effect sizes are large and durable. The blood-injection-injury subtype is uniquely characterised by a biphasic autonomic response (sympathetic spike then parasympathetic-mediated vasovagal drop and syncope), and Ost's applied tension technique (tensing large muscle groups to maintain BP during exposure) is the targeted modification. Pharmacotherapy is rarely indicated.
key_points: Specific phobia: graded in-vivo exposure first-line
Blood-injection-injury subtype: add applied tension
Biphasic autonomic response in BII subtype is unique
Pharmacotherapy rarely indicated
Effect sizes for exposure therapy are large and durable
explanation.pearls: Applied tension: tense leg, arm, and trunk muscles for 10-15 sec then release; repeat several times; teaches the body to maintain BP during exposure.

subtopic_detail: Blood-injection-injury phobia and applied tension

stem: A 41-year-old man develops a 12-month pattern of fear and avoidance of open spaces, public transport, supermarkets, and being far from home alone, after recovering from an initial 6-month period of panic disorder. He has not had a discrete panic attack for the last 4 months but cannot leave his suburb without his wife.

data_table.Examination: Anxious about appointment topic; otherwise unremarkable.
data_table.Investigations: Nil acute required.

lead_in: Which is the most accurate DSM-5 diagnosis now?

options.text: Agoraphobia (a separate DSM-5 diagnosis from panic disorder)
options.rationale: DSM-5 separates agoraphobia from panic disorder (they were combined in DSM-IV-TR). Agoraphobia is now diagnosed independently when there is fear or avoidance of two or more situations from a specified list (public transport, open spaces, enclosed spaces, crowds/queues, being outside home alone) due to fear of difficulty escaping or being helped if panic-like or other incapacitating symptoms occur, with avoidance >=6 months.

options.text: Social anxiety disorder
options.rationale: Fear here is of being unable to escape or be helped, not of social scrutiny; this is not social anxiety disorder. Would be wrong (Source: DSM-5-TR).

options.text: Specific phobia, situational type
options.rationale: Agoraphobia is its own diagnosis with multiple situations (>=2 from list); specific phobia is one circumscribed object/situation. Would be wrong (Source: DSM-5-TR).

options.text: Panic disorder is the only diagnosis given prior attacks
options.rationale: In DSM-5, panic disorder and agoraphobia are separate diagnoses; current absence of panic attacks for 4 months does not retain panic disorder as the active diagnosis. Would be wrong (Source: DSM-5-TR).

options.text: Generalised anxiety disorder
options.rationale: Worry pattern here is situational not multi-domain free-floating; GAD does not fit. Would be wrong (Source: DSM-5-TR).

explanation.summary: Agoraphobia in DSM-5 is a standalone diagnosis (separated from panic disorder, which was the DSM-IV-TR coupling). It requires fear or avoidance of >=2 of the specified situations due to fear of escape difficulty or being unable to be helped if panic-like or incapacitating symptoms occur, lasting >=6 months. Treatment is exposure-based CBT plus SSRI for moderate-severe cases.
key_points: DSM-5: agoraphobia is standalone (was coupled in DSM-IV-TR)
Avoidance of >=2 specified situations, >=6 months
Fear of escape difficulty or helplessness, not social scrutiny
Treatment: exposure-based CBT + SSRI for moderate-severe
Can coexist with or follow panic disorder
explanation.pearls: About 30-50% of patients with panic disorder develop agoraphobia; recognising the transition guides escalation of exposure work.

subtopic_detail: Agoraphobia as standalone DSM-5 diagnosis

stem: A 36-year-old woman attends her GP at a Hunter New England LHD primary care practice for the eighth time this year, requesting investigation of breast lumps that on examination and recent ultrasound are normal cyclical changes. She spends hours daily checking her body, researching cancer symptoms online, seeking reassurance from her partner, and avoiding news stories about cancer. She has minimal somatic symptoms but persistent fear of having a serious illness. Reassurance from prior negative investigations provides relief for hours to days before fear returns.

data_table.Examination: Bilateral cyclical breast changes confirmed by ultrasound 2 months ago; otherwise normal.
data_table.Investigations: Recent bloods, mammogram and ultrasound normal.

lead_in: Which is the most likely DSM-5 diagnosis?

options.text: Illness anxiety disorder
options.rationale: DSM-5 illness anxiety disorder describes preoccupation with having or acquiring a serious illness, with mild or absent somatic symptoms, high health anxiety, excessive health-related behaviours (or maladaptive avoidance), and persistence >=6 months. Contrasts with somatic symptom disorder (prominent somatic symptoms drive the picture) and OCD (ego-dystonic intrusive thoughts with ritualised neutralising).
source_refs: Tyrer P et al. Lancet 2014 health anxiety review

options.text: Somatic symptom disorder
options.rationale: Somatic symptom disorder requires prominent distressing somatic symptoms; this patient's somatic findings are minimal/normal and the picture is dominated by fear. Would be wrong (Source: DSM-5-TR).

options.text: Obsessive-compulsive disorder
options.rationale: Although there is checking and reassurance-seeking, the intrusions are ego-syntonic (worry about illness) rather than ego-dystonic and bizarre; classification as illness anxiety disorder is more accurate. Would be less precise (Source: DSM-5-TR).

options.text: Generalised anxiety disorder
options.rationale: GAD requires multi-domain worry; here the worry is focused on a single domain (health). Would be wrong (Source: DSM-5-TR).

options.text: Body dysmorphic disorder
options.rationale: BDD focuses on perceived flaws in physical appearance, not on having a serious illness. Would be wrong (Source: DSM-5-TR).

explanation.summary: Illness anxiety disorder (DSM-5) replaced much of what was previously called hypochondriasis. Characterised by preoccupation with illness, minimal somatic symptoms, high health anxiety, and excessive checking/reassurance-seeking or avoidance. Care-seeking and care-avoidant subtypes exist. Treatment is CBT focused on reducing reassurance-seeking and avoidance; SSRIs have a secondary role.
key_points: Illness anxiety disorder: preoccupation with illness, minimal somatic symptoms
Somatic symptom disorder: prominent somatic symptoms drive picture
Reassurance gives short-term relief - reinforces the pattern
Treatment: CBT, reduce reassurance-seeking
SSRIs second-line
explanation.pearls: Schedule a single trusted GP for all health concerns with planned regular reviews to reduce ED and ad-hoc reassurance-seeking; this is itself therapeutic.

subtopic_detail: Illness anxiety disorder vs SSD vs OCD discrimination

stem: A 42-year-old woman with newly diagnosed panic disorder presents to her GP at a Central Coast LHD clinic. The GP plans to start sertraline 25 mg daily, increasing to 50 mg after a week, and warns her about an initial worsening of anxiety in the first 1-2 weeks. She asks whether anything can help her get through that period.

data_table.Examination: Anxious; HR 92, BP 122/78.
data_table.Investigations: Nil acute.

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate brief adjunctive strategy?

options.text: Diazepam 5 mg PRN with explicit limit of 2 weeks supply, plus dispensed information on the time-limited nature
options.rationale: A short-course benzodiazepine bridge (<= 2-4 weeks) is supported by Australian guidance for managing the initial SSRI-induced anxiety paradox in panic disorder, provided it is time-limited, explicitly framed as bridging, and dispensed with explicit cessation planning. PRN dosing reduces total exposure. Long-term maintenance benzodiazepines are not the goal.

options.text: Diazepam 10 mg three times daily for 3 months
options.rationale: Three months of scheduled high-dose benzodiazepine creates tolerance and dependence; the bridge is brief. Would be wrong (Source: eTG).

options.text: Stop sertraline if anxiety worsens at any point in the first 2 weeks
options.rationale: Stopping in the first 1-2 weeks loses the eventual benefit; the patient should be prepared for transient worsening, not abandoning the treatment. Would be wrong (Source: AMH).

options.text: Add propranolol 40 mg daily long-term
options.rationale: Propranolol addresses peripheral autonomic symptoms only; not a panic-disorder maintenance or bridging strategy. Would be wrong (Source: AMH).

options.text: Add quetiapine 50 mg nocte
options.rationale: Off-label, sedating, metabolic risk, not the appropriate bridge. Would be wrong (Source: AMH).

explanation.summary: Initial SSRI anxiety paradox is a common early experience in anxiety disorder treatment, peaking in week 1 and resolving by 2-4 weeks. Strategies: (1) start at half-dose for the first week; (2) warn the patient; (3) consider a time-limited benzodiazepine bridge (<= 2-4 weeks) with explicit cessation planning; (4) maintain SSRI through the paradox window. Long-term benzodiazepine maintenance is not the goal.
key_points: Start SSRI at half the depression dose in anxiety
Warn about anxiety paradox in week 1
Brief benzodiazepine bridge OK (<= 2-4 weeks) with cessation planning
Maintain SSRI through the paradox window
Long-term benzodiazepines avoided
explanation.pearls: Explicit framing - 'this medication temporarily increases anxiety before reducing it' - reduces premature discontinuation, which is the most common cause of SSRI failure in anxiety.

subtopic_detail: Time-limited benzodiazepine bridge during SSRI initiation

stem: A 30-year-old woman has had OCD (Y-BOCS 28) for 10 years. She has had adequate trials (12 weeks at maximum dose) of sertraline 200 mg, fluoxetine 80 mg, and clomipramine 225 mg, with minimal response. She is concurrently engaged with ERP. The psychiatrist is considering antipsychotic augmentation.

data_table.Examination: Persistent contamination and harm obsessions with checking and washing.
data_table.Investigations: ECG sinus rhythm, QTc 425 ms; metabolic panel normal.

lead_in: Which augmentation strategy has the strongest evidence in treatment-resistant OCD?

options.text: Add a low-dose atypical antipsychotic (risperidone 0.5-1 mg or aripiprazole 5-10 mg) and reassess at 6-8 weeks
options.rationale: After adequate trials of SSRI and clomipramine without response, low-dose atypical antipsychotic augmentation is the strongest evidence-based pharmacological augmentation in treatment-resistant OCD. Risperidone and aripiprazole have the most positive meta-analytic data (Komossa et al, Veale et al). Response is assessed at 6-8 weeks; non-responders should have augmentation withdrawn to avoid long-term metabolic risk.
source_refs: Veale D et al. Atypical augmentation in OCD meta-analysis 2014

options.text: Commence regular clonazepam 0.5 mg twice daily as anxiety-targeted augmentation and reassess at 6-8 weeks
options.rationale: Benzodiazepines do not augment OCD response and may impair ERP learning by blunting habituation; not an evidence-based augmentation strategy (Source: NICE OCD CG31).

options.text: Withdraw all pharmacotherapy and rely on intensive exposure and response prevention monotherapy for 12 weeks
options.rationale: In severe treatment-resistant OCD with Y-BOCS 28 monotherapy ERP is unlikely to be sufficient; combined pharmacotherapy plus ERP is recommended (Source: RANZCP).

options.text: Commence the irreversible MAOI phenelzine 15 mg three times daily with tyramine dietary counselling and reassess at 8 weeks
options.rationale: MAOIs do not have OCD evidence and carry dietary and drug-interaction burden; not an augmentation option in resistant OCD (Source: AMH).

options.text: Refer for a course of bilateral electroconvulsive therapy (6 to 12 sessions) with anaesthetic review and reassess at 8 weeks
options.rationale: ECT lacks evidence in primary OCD and is reserved for severe comorbid depression that has not responded to other treatments (Source: RANZCP).

explanation.summary: In treatment-resistant OCD (adequate trial failure of SSRI + clomipramine + ERP), low-dose atypical antipsychotic augmentation is the most evidence-supported pharmacological step. Risperidone and aripiprazole have positive meta-analytic data; the response should be reassessed at 6-8 weeks and the augmentation withdrawn if no response, due to metabolic burden. Other neuromodulation options (deep brain stimulation, transcranial magnetic stimulation) are reserved for severe refractory cases.
key_points: Treatment-resistant OCD: adequate trials of SSRI + clomipramine without response
First augmentation: atypical antipsychotic (risperidone, aripiprazole) low dose
Reassess at 6-8 weeks; withdraw if no response
rTMS approved by TGA for OCD as adjunct
DBS reserved for severe refractory
explanation.pearls: Approximately 30-40% of OCD patients meet treatment-resistance criteria; the bar is high and adequate dose + duration is often the missing piece before declaring resistance.

sources.label: Veale D et al. Atypical augmentation in OCD meta-analysis BMC Psychiatry 2014

subtopic_detail: Atypical antipsychotic augmentation in treatment-resistant OCD

stem: A 14-year-old presents to a Hunter New England LHD adolescent mental health clinic with a 14-month history of moderate-severe generalised anxiety. She has completed an evidence-based CBT programme (BRAVE) with partial benefit; GAD-7 remains 14. Her parents and the patient agree to consider pharmacotherapy.

data_table.Examination: Anxious-appearing; mental state otherwise unremarkable.
data_table.Investigations: TFT, FBC normal.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate first-line pharmacological choice for adolescent anxiety in Australia?

options.text: Fluoxetine 10 mg daily, titrating cautiously with close early review
options.rationale: Fluoxetine has the strongest paediatric trial evidence in adolescent anxiety and depression and is supported by TGA-aware Australian guidance (eTG, RANZCP) as first-line in this age group. Sertraline is an equally evidence-supported alternative. The TGA Black Triangle suicidality advisory applies in under-25s: close early review (within 1-2 weeks, then weekly through the first month) is required.
source_refs: eTG Psychotropic: Anxiety in young people

options.text: Diazepam 2 mg three times daily
options.rationale: Benzodiazepines are not appropriate first-line for adolescent anxiety and carry abuse and dependence concerns. Would be wrong (Source: eTG).

options.text: Olanzapine 5 mg nocte
options.rationale: Antipsychotics are not first-line for adolescent anxiety; metabolic and movement disorder risks substantial. Would be wrong (Source: AMH).

options.text: Amitriptyline 25 mg nocte
options.rationale: Tricyclics have poor evidence in paediatric anxiety, worse tolerability and lethality in overdose. Would be wrong (Source: AMH).

options.text: Pregabalin 75 mg twice daily
options.rationale: Pregabalin is not first-line in adolescents and has abuse potential; not appropriate. Would be wrong (Source: eTG).

explanation.summary: In adolescent anxiety disorders, fluoxetine and sertraline are first-line pharmacotherapy when CBT has been inadequate or as combination with CBT for moderate-severe presentations. Fluoxetine has the longest paediatric trial evidence. The TGA Black Triangle advisory mandates close early review for suicidality. Combined CBT + SSRI outperforms either alone in paediatric anxiety (CAMS study).
key_points: First-line in adolescents: fluoxetine or sertraline
Combine with CBT for moderate-severe presentations
TGA Black Triangle close monitoring under-25s
Start low, titrate slowly
Avoid benzodiazepines and tricyclics in this age group
explanation.pearls: The Child/Adolescent Anxiety Multimodal Study (CAMS, NEJM 2008) showed combined CBT + sertraline > either alone for paediatric anxiety, with 80% response on combined therapy vs 60% on each monotherapy.

sources.label: Walkup JT et al. CAMS Study. NEJM 2008

subtopic_detail: First-line pharmacotherapy for adolescent anxiety

stem: A 7-year-old previously well child is brought to a Hunter New England LHD paediatric clinic by his mother with an abrupt onset over 2-3 days of severe contamination obsessions, hand washing, and new motor tics. Two weeks ago he had pharyngitis treated with phenoxymethylpenicillin. There is no family history of OCD or tic disorder. On examination he has subtle choreiform movements of the fingers when distracted.

data_table.Examination: Subtle choreiform movements, no fever, no cardiac murmur, no joint signs.
data_table.Investigations: Throat swab pending; ASO and anti-DNase B titres pending.

lead_in: Which is the most likely diagnostic framework, and what is the recommended first step?

options.text: PANDAS (paediatric autoimmune neuropsychiatric disorder associated with streptococcal infection); refer to a paediatric specialist for streptococcal serology, throat swab, and review for any cardiac/joint involvement before considering immunomodulatory therapy
options.rationale: Abrupt-onset prepubertal OCD or tics within weeks of a streptococcal infection is the classic PANDAS phenotype (Swedo's criteria: prepubertal onset, abrupt sudden, episodic course, temporal association with strep, presence of OCD or tics, neurological abnormalities). Diagnostic workup includes throat swab + serology and assessment for sequelae of acute rheumatic fever (carditis, arthritis). Immunomodulation is reserved for selected cases under specialist guidance; SSRIs and CBT remain core for OCD symptoms.
source_refs: Swedo SE et al. PANDAS criteria 1998

options.text: First-episode psychosis presenting with abrupt-onset obsessions and motor tics in a 7-year-old; admit for inpatient antipsychotic initiation and observation despite the absence of thought disorder, hallucinations, or any other positive psychotic feature on examination
options.rationale: Abrupt OCD with new tics in a 7-year-old is not first-episode psychosis; no thought disorder or hallucinations are described and the temporal link to streptococcal infection supports an autoimmune framework (Source: DSM-5-TR).

options.text: Tourette syndrome with new motor tics and obsessive features; reassure the family that no further investigation is required, ignore the temporal link to recent pharyngitis, and review in the paediatric clinic in 6 months for tic progression
options.rationale: Tourette syndrome is chronic with insidious onset and varied tics rather than abrupt-onset OCD plus new tics following pharyngitis; pattern does not fit (Source: DSM-5-TR).

options.text: Childhood-onset primary obsessive-compulsive disorder; start sertraline 25 mg orally daily without further investigation of the recent streptococcal infection or any assessment for acute rheumatic fever sequelae such as carditis or arthritis
options.rationale: Abrupt post-infectious onset with new tics warrants PANDAS-framework workup including throat swab, serology and acute-rheumatic-fever screening before defaulting to standard OCD pathway (Source: Swedo).

options.text: Sydenham chorea as the primary diagnostic label; arrange immediate neurology admission, commence empirical immunomodulation with intravenous immunoglobulin pending serological confirmation, and defer streptococcal serology to the inpatient workup
options.rationale: Sydenham chorea typically has more prominent generalised chorea, often with carditis; the predominant feature here is OCD with subtle movement findings, which fits PANDAS more precisely (Source: RACP).

explanation.summary: PANDAS (Swedo et al 1998) describes abrupt prepubertal-onset OCD and/or tics within weeks of group A streptococcal infection, with episodic course, neurological abnormalities (often choreiform movements), and improvement after the infection. PANS (paediatric acute-onset neuropsychiatric syndrome) is a broader umbrella with non-streptococcal triggers. Diagnostic workup: throat swab, ASO and anti-DNase B titres, screen for acute rheumatic fever sequelae. Management: treat any active infection, standard OCD treatment (SSRI + CBT), with immunomodulation in selected severe cases under specialist guidance. Sydenham chorea (a major Jones criterion for ARF) is a key differential.
key_points: Swedo PANDAS criteria: prepubertal abrupt OCD/tics + strep + episodic + neurological signs
PANS = broader non-strep umbrella
Workup: throat swab + ASO/anti-DNase B + ARF screen
Standard OCD therapy core; immunomodulation in selected cases
Sydenham chorea is the key differential and Jones criterion
explanation.pearls: About 5-10% of childhood OCD cases may fit PANDAS criteria; recognition matters because missing acute rheumatic fever has cardiac consequences.

sources.label: Swedo SE et al. PANDAS. Am J Psychiatry 1998

subtopic_detail: PANDAS framework for abrupt post-strep OCD with tics

stem: A GP at a Hunter New England LHD primary care practice wants to introduce a standardised screening and severity tool for generalised anxiety disorder. She asks a Y4 medical student for the most widely used short instrument.

data_table.Examination: N/A teaching scenario.
data_table.Investigations: N/A.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate tool?

options.text: GAD-7 (Generalised Anxiety Disorder 7-item scale)
options.rationale: GAD-7 (Spitzer et al 2006) is the most widely used brief self-report screen and severity measure for GAD in primary care, with a cut-off of >=10 indicating likely GAD. It is short (7 items), validated, free, and forms part of the Better Access initiative and many electronic templates in Australia.
source_refs: Spitzer RL et al. GAD-7. Arch Intern Med 2006

options.text: Beck Depression Inventory II (21 items, depression)
options.rationale: BDI-II measures depressive symptom severity and is not a screen for generalised anxiety disorder (Source: Beck 1996).

options.text: Yale-Brown Obsessive Compulsive Scale (10 items)
options.rationale: Y-BOCS is specific to OCD symptom severity and is not a generalised anxiety screen (Source: Goodman 1989).

options.text: PTSD Checklist for DSM-5 (PCL-5, 20 items)
options.rationale: PCL-5 is the validated PTSD checklist for DSM-5 and is not a generalised anxiety screen (Source: Weathers 2013).

options.text: Edinburgh Postnatal Depression Scale (EPDS)
options.rationale: EPDS is for postnatal depression screening rather than generalised anxiety disorder (Source: Cox 1987).

explanation.summary: Common short instruments by disorder: GAD-7 for GAD, PHQ-9 for depression, PCL-5 for PTSD, Y-BOCS for OCD severity, EPDS for postnatal depression. The GAD-7 cut-off of >=10 has sensitivity 89% and specificity 82% for GAD.
key_points: GAD-7: screening + severity for GAD; cut-off >=10
PHQ-9: depression
PCL-5: PTSD
Y-BOCS: OCD severity
EPDS: postnatal depression
explanation.pearls: GAD-7 is sensitive to other anxiety disorders too (panic, social, PTSD) - high score warrants further differential clarification, not automatic GAD label.

sources.label: Spitzer RL et al. GAD-7. Arch Intern Med 2006

subtopic_detail: Standard GAD screening tool: GAD-7

stem: A 21-year-old man attends a Hunter New England LHD GP requesting a referral for rhinoplasty. He spends 3-4 hours daily examining his nose in mirrors, has avoided his university classes for 6 months because he is convinced his nose is grotesque, and has had three previous surgical consultations with different cosmetic surgeons who found his nose normal-appearing. He has tried mirror covering and avoidance of photographs.

data_table.Examination: Normal-appearing nasal anatomy; mood low, distress around appearance topic high.
data_table.Investigations: Nil acute.

lead_in: Which is the most likely diagnosis and what is the most appropriate management approach?

options.text: Body dysmorphic disorder; refer for CBT (specifically targeted at BDD) and consider an SSRI; avoid the cosmetic procedure
options.rationale: BDD is characterised by preoccupation with perceived flaws in appearance that are not observable or appear slight to others, repetitive behaviours (mirror checking, comparing) or mental acts in response, and clinically significant distress or impairment. CBT specifically adapted to BDD and SSRI (often at higher doses, similar to OCD) are first-line. Cosmetic procedures typically do not relieve symptoms and may worsen them; rates of dissatisfaction post-procedure exceed 70%.

options.text: Schizophrenia with somatic delusions; admit for antipsychotic initiation
options.rationale: BDD with poor insight can resemble delusional preoccupation but typically lacks broader psychotic features; the differential is BDD with absent insight, not schizophrenia. Would be over-medicalising. Would be wrong (Source: DSM-5-TR).

options.text: Refer for rhinoplasty as requested to relieve distress
options.rationale: Cosmetic procedures rarely relieve BDD distress; >70% report worse symptoms post-procedure. Would be wrong (Source: Phillips 2015).

options.text: Generalised anxiety disorder; start escitalopram
options.rationale: GAD is multi-domain worry; this is focused single-domain appearance preoccupation with checking, fitting BDD. Would be wrong on diagnostic label (Source: DSM-5-TR).

options.text: Illness anxiety disorder; reassure and review in 6 months
options.rationale: IAD concerns having or acquiring a serious illness, not perceived flaws in appearance. Would be wrong (Source: DSM-5-TR).

explanation.summary: BDD is in the OCD-spectrum (DSM-5). Treatment is CBT specifically adapted to BDD (with exposure and response prevention to mirror-checking, reassurance-seeking and avoidance) plus SSRI (often at higher OCD-style doses). Cosmetic procedures rarely help and often worsen the preoccupation, shifting it to another body part. Insight can be poor; the specifier 'with absent insight/delusional beliefs' applies when conviction is firm but does not change first-line treatment.
key_points: BDD: preoccupation with perceived flaw + repetitive behaviours
First-line: CBT for BDD + SSRI
Cosmetic procedures rarely help, often harm
Insight can be poor (specifier in DSM-5)
Higher SSRI doses similar to OCD
explanation.pearls: Suicide risk in BDD is markedly elevated (lifetime ~80% ideation, ~25% attempts); screening is essential.

subtopic_detail: BDD diagnosis and CBT+SSRI management; avoid cosmetic procedures
